metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether tracker_head_sha() should treat untracked-only ('??') porcelain output as a failure or limit dirty detection to tracked-file changes (M, D, A, UU prefixes), and verify this matches the intended cleanliness contract for the tickets tracker.
evidence: src/rebar/signing.py:329-336 — existing pattern uses non-empty porcelain output as dirty marker, but only for the source tree

Cache finding 1b7165e48 confirms: 'tracker_head_sha's any output raises store-read-failure rule applied to git status --porcelain will fire on untracked files (?? lines), causing false store-read-failures'
Plan: 'any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure)' — no carve-out for ?? lines
findings.finding: The plan specifies that tracker_head_sha() raises store-read-failure when 'git status --porcelain' produces ANY output, without acknowledging that untracked files (reported as '??' lines) are benign in a tickets tracker worktree and would trigger false failures. The existing usage of this pattern in signing.py (line 330-336) is scoped to the rebar source tree where untracked files are controlled; the tickets tracker is a different context where temp files, editor swap files, or .cache.json could appear as untracked without indicating a dirty/unsafe state.
findings.location: Implementation § tracker_head_sha: 'run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain … any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError'
scenarios: A tracker directory with an untracked temp file (e.g., editor .swp, OS .DS_Store) causes every plan-review signing attempt to fail with store-read-failure even though the tracked state is clean.
findings.suggested_fix: Limit the dirty check to lines that do NOT start with '??' (i.e., filter for actual tracked-file modifications), consistent with how ls-files -u already handles the unmerged case separately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the 'any output from git status --porcelain raises store-read-failure' rule should filter out untracked-file ('??') lines, or confirm the tickets tracker is guaranteed to contain zero untracked files and document that guarantee as a precondition of tracker_head_sha().
evidence: git status --porcelain output for untracked files: '?? <filename>' — any-output rule fires on these lines
tests/conftest.py lines 388-414: the test suite's own use of git status --porcelain explicitly handles the '??' case and filters net-new entries rather than rejecting any output
The tickets tracker is an orphan-branch worktree; its cleanliness guarantee is not stated in the plan
'Dirty, unmerged, subprocess, IO, path, or malformed-output failures map to store-read-failure' — 'dirty' is undefined as modified/staged-only or including untracked
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'git -C <tracker> status --porcelain; any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure)' but git status --porcelain reports untracked files as '?? <path>' lines in addition to modified/staged files. If the tickets tracker working tree contains any untracked files (e.g. a .rebar/ cache dir, a tmp file, or any file not tracked by the orphan branch), this rule will permanently fail all plan-review signing with store-read-failure even when the tracker is functionally clean. The plan does not specify whether untracked-file lines should be filtered out or whether the tracker is guaranteed to have none.
findings.location: Implementation section — tracker_head_sha() spec: 'run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain … any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure)'
scenarios: A developer's local tracker has an untracked .DS_Store or editor backup file → every plan-review sign attempt returns INDETERMINATE missing-target until the file is manually removed
A CI environment mounts additional files into the tracker worktree → signing fails silently for all tickets
The plan's own example of 'dirty' state is ambiguous: the git index considers untracked files neither dirty nor clean — only the working tree is 'dirty' in the modified/staged sense
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the porcelain check to index-dirty lines only by filtering out lines starting with '??' (or equivalently use 'git status --porcelain=v1' and reject only lines whose first two characters are not '??'), OR document that the tracker worktree must have a .gitignore that excludes all untracked content and add that as a precondition comment in tracker_head_sha().

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit named scenario descriptions for duplicate-edge collapse (AC 7) and bidirectional prerequisite normalization (AC 8) to the test_plan_relation_snapshot.py coverage claim in the Verification section.
evidence: 'proves canonical collection, archived inbound sources, tracker HEAD capture, every exact abort reason, structured logs, and pre-LLM termination' — neither duplicate-edge collapse nor bidirectional normalization is named explicitly
AC 7: 'Duplicate edges collapse within each role'
AC 8: 'Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite'
findings.finding: ACs 7 ('Duplicate edges collapse within each role') and 8 ('Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite') have no explicitly named scenario in the Verification section; 'proves canonical collection' is the only description given for test_plan_relation_snapshot.py, and an executor could write tests that exercise single-direction, deduplication-free cases and still consider the description satisfied, leaving these two ACs without a concrete proving scenario.
findings.location: Verification section — 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_relation_snapshot.py proves canonical collection…'
scenarios: Executor writes a test with one depends_on edge and one blocks edge (both for the same target) but counts two pins instead of one, or writes a test that only exercises depends_on and skips the inbound-blocks direction; both tests could pass while the ACs remain unmet.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Verification bullet to read: '…proves canonical collection (including duplicate-edge collapse within each role and both depends_on/blocks directions normalising to role prerequisite), archived inbound sources, tracker HEAD capture, every exact abort reason, structured logs, and pre-LLM termination.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out procedure to the plan that covers the case where child 75de's enforcement flag is not yet deployed, including how pre-pin code handles existing pin-bearing manifests (point to the pre-feature-verifier fixture result as the safety proof).
evidence: 'enforcement is disabled before rollback and the next forward review restores pins' — implies the 75de flag is a prerequisite for the described rollback sequence
'Child 75de owns the boolean verify.enforce_plan_material_pins key' — 75de is a later child story
'The feature branch is not merged or deployed between these stories' — so 75de may not be deployed when rollback of this story is needed
No step-by-step rollback procedure is stated (e.g., 'set enforce_plan_material_pins=false, then redeploy prior image')
findings.finding: The rollback scenario is acknowledged as producing acceptable metadata loss but no explicit back-out procedure is given; the only named mechanism ('enforcement is disabled before rollback') depends on child 75de's flag, which is implemented after this story — creating a rollback window where the flag does not yet exist.
findings.location: Implementation paragraph 6 ('On an operational rollback to pre-pin code...')
scenarios: If this story's code is deployed and must be rolled back before child 75de is merged, the 'disable enforcement before rollback' step cannot be performed because the flag does not exist yet, leaving operators without a clear back-out path.
Operators triggering rollback for an unrelated reason might not know that pin-bearing manifests produced during the forward deployment remain in place and how pre-pin code handles them.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: '(1) If 75de is deployed: set verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false, then redeploy prior image. (2) If 75de is not yet deployed: redeploy prior image directly — pre-pin code authenticates pin-bearing manifests over full DSSE bytes and ignores pin lines for field interpretation (proven by the pre-feature-verifier fixture); metadata loss is reconstructible on next forward review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one structured log line on the success path of `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` (e.g., `{event: plan_relation_snapshot_collected, ticket_id, child_count, prerequisite_count, ticket_store_revision, duration_ms}`) so operators can observe and diagnose the new mandatory pre-sign phase.
evidence: Plan specifies structured ERROR record: {event: plan_relation_snapshot_error, reason: <enum>, canonical_id: string|null, reference: string|null}
ABSENCE: no success-path log, metric, or trace specified for collect_plan_relation_snapshot or tracker_head_sha
Plan states 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states' — an O(store) operation with no latency instrumentation
findings.finding: The new mandatory pre-sign phase (`collect_plan_relation_snapshot`) scans the entire ticket store and executes subprocess git calls on every signing operation, but the plan specifies observability only for the ERROR path (structured plan_relation_snapshot_error event). There is no logging, metric, or trace specified for the SUCCESS path — operators cannot observe latency, collection size, or slow git calls in normal operation.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha', 'collect_plan_relation_snapshot'; Acceptance Criteria; Verification
scenarios: The ticket store grows large and every sign/refresh/re-sign operation silently slows down; operators have no signal to diagnose the bottleneck.

A local git repository develops an index lock or slow fsync; tracker_head_sha hangs but only the eventual store-read-failure error (if it eventually fires) is visible — no early warning or duration log.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a debug-level structured log at the end of collect_plan_relation_snapshot with fields: ticket_id, child_count, prerequisite_count, ticket_store_revision (first 8 hex), duration_ms. This satisfies the observability check without adding new dependencies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended behavior for unexpected exceptions from collect_plan_relation_snapshot (e.g., 'any exception not already a PlanRelationSnapshotError is wrapped as store-read-failure before the boundary handler fires') so the executor has a concrete default to implement.
evidence: 'The review boundary catches it before any LLM call' — only PlanRelationSnapshotError is named as the caught type
'the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback' — this invariant is broken if unexpected exceptions propagate as tracebacks
No statement that unexpected exceptions are re-wrapped or suppressed
findings.finding: The plan specifies that PlanRelationSnapshotError is caught at the review boundary and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE, but does not state the intended behavior when collect_plan_relation_snapshot raises an unexpected exception (e.g., RuntimeError, AttributeError) — the claim 'the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback' implies all exceptions are caught, yet the plan only names PlanRelationSnapshotError in the catch logic.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'The review boundary catches it before any LLM call, emits an ERROR record exactly {…}, and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE…; the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback.'
scenarios: An internal bug in collect_plan_relation_snapshot raises AttributeError; the review boundary does not catch it; a traceback reaches the caller, violating the stated invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'The review boundary wraps any non-PlanRelationSnapshotError exception from collect_plan_relation_snapshot as PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure) before applying the structured-error path, preserving the no-traceback invariant.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion explicitly covering the attest.py re-export of ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() (e.g., 'attest.py exposes ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() alongside existing manifest helpers').
evidence: 'attest.py imports and re-exports both beside the existing manifest helpers' — Implementation section, no corresponding AC line
No AC reads: 'attest.py re-exports ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins()'
Verification says 'test_attest_opcert.py proves ... attest.py re-exports' but the AC set contains no criterion to anchor this test result
findings.finding: The described deliverable 'attest.py imports and re-exports both [ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins()] beside the existing manifest helpers' has no covering acceptance criterion; only the Verification section (test_attest_opcert.py 'proves attest.py re-exports') exercises it.
findings.location: Implementation paragraph 1 ('Define ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() in manifest.py; attest.py imports and re-exports both') and Verification section
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm acceptance of the re-export deliverable from the AC checklist alone; a regression in the re-export surface would not be caught by any AC-level gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'attest.py re-exports ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() so callers need not import from manifest.py directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that tracker_head_sha() delegates to the existing `run_git` helper from `rebar._store.gitutil` rather than re-implementing subprocess invocation, consistent with the consolidation documented in CHANGELOG line 384.
evidence: src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py:26 — run_git() is the shared git subprocess wrapper used across store, snapshot, code_review layers
src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py:25 — 'from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git' proves it is importable at the llm/ layer
Plan specifies tracker_head_sha catches 'Missing git, invalid paths, nonzero subprocess exits, IO errors' — the same contract run_git already handles
Three independent git subprocess re-implementations already consolidated by CHANGELOG.md:384 — 'Consolidate the _git() subprocess wrappers into gitutil.run_git'
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py rebuilds git subprocess logic already provided by the production rebar._store.gitutil.run_git wrapper, which is already imported and used at the llm/ layer (rebar.llm.code_review.assemble imports run_git directly). The plan specifies that tracker_head_sha must invoke 'git -C <tracker> status --porcelain', 'git -C <tracker> ls-files -u', and 'git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD', catching missing-git, IO errors, invalid paths, and nonzero exits — exactly the contract run_git provides. This is a NIH rebuild of a production git helper that is importable from relation_snapshot.py.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain...'
scenarios: Any divergence in error-catching semantics between tracker_head_sha and run_git will introduce a maintenance inconsistency across git callers
If run_git gains new timeout or environment behavior, tracker_head_sha won't inherit it
findings.suggested_fix: Implement tracker_head_sha() using run_git(tracker, 'status', '--porcelain', check=False), run_git(tracker, 'ls-files', '-u', check=False), and run_git(tracker, 'rev-parse', 'HEAD', check=False), adapting the CompletedProcess results locally — the same pattern already used in rebar.llm.code_review.assemble._git().

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that PlanRelationSnapshot's promotion to its own module (with full error hierarchy) is justified now, or consider inlining the snapshot logic into the sign path until child 7c27 is concrete enough to validate the boundary.
evidence: Plan explicitly says 'Child 7c27 owns registration, schemas, prompts, and routing for the future prerequisite criterion; this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists'
No existing call-sites for PlanRelationSnapshot found in codebase search
The new module introduces four new public symbols (PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, tracker_head_sha) for a single in-story consumer
Rule-of-Three requires >=3 existing call-sites before a new abstraction is warranted
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshot is introduced as a new named typed abstraction in its own module (plan_review/relation_snapshot.py) with one in-scope call-site (pin emission in the signing paths) and one explicitly-not-yet-built future call-site (child 7c27's prerequisite criterion). The plan states 'this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists.' At story completion, only one real call-site exists, which does not meet the Rule-of-Three threshold for a non-trivial new module with its own error hierarchy.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py'
scenarios: Child 7c27 may be restructured or deferred, leaving the typed boundary as dead structure
The boundary adds indirection without a current multi-consumer justification

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload is built by delegating to `reduce_all_tickets(exclude_archived=False)` (the established pattern) rather than a bespoke inline reduction loop, since the archived-source filter is in find_inbound_relationships — not in reduce_all_tickets.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_api.py:215 — reduce_all_tickets(exclude_archived=False) already reduces every ticket directory including archived
src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py:103 — the archived filter is in find_inbound_relationships, not in reduce_all_tickets
The plan's rationale for bypassing find_inbound_relationships is the archived-source filter, but that does not require bypassing reduce_all_tickets
Multiple existing call-sites already use reduce_all_tickets(exclude_archived=False) for store-wide preloads (graph/_hierarchy.py:238, graph/_unblock.py:130)
findings.finding: The O(store) full-preload of every ticket directory (including archived) to build ticket_states_by_id reimplements the behavior of the existing reduce_all_tickets(exclude_archived=False) call. The plan rejects find_inbound_relationships because its 'active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets', but that filter is in _inbound.py's reduce step — not in reduce_all_tickets itself. The preload needed for pin collection (all states by ID) could be satisfied by reduce_all_tickets(exclude_archived=False) rather than a bespoke inline O(store) reduction loop.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states; this explicit O(store) map is required to discover arbitrary inbound blockers.'
scenarios: Bespoke preload loop may diverge from reduce_all_tickets's .archived marker self-heal logic and orphan-marker handling
Maintenance: future changes to reduce_all_tickets (e.g. exclude_session_logs optimization) won't propagate to the bespoke loop
findings.suggested_fix: Call `{s['ticket_id']: s for s in reduce_all_tickets(tracker_dir, exclude_archived=False) if isinstance(s, dict) and s.get('ticket_id')}` to build ticket_states_by_id, reusing the existing batch reducer with its caching and self-heal behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` as an explicit edit target in the Implementation section, specifying where `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` is called before `attest.sign_plan_review`.
evidence: Plan text: 'resign_plan_review calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot immediately before building its new manifest'
resign.py confirmed at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py; calls attest.sign_plan_review at line 126 — must be wired to collect_plan_relation_snapshot first
resign.py never mentioned as an edit target in the Implementation section
findings.finding: resign.py is a required edit target that the plan never names: the plan mandates that resign_plan_review calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot before building its manifest, but resign.py (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py) is not listed anywhere as a file to be modified.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'Every plan-review signature-producing path — ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review'
scenarios: An implementor following only the named edit targets would miss adding the collect_plan_relation_snapshot call in resign_plan_review, leaving re-signs pinless.
AC 19 ('Re-signing emits the complete current pin set') would silently fail if resign.py is not wired.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add a bullet: 'Edit resign.py: call collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root) before attest.sign_plan_review; on PlanRelationSnapshotError return the same unsigned INDETERMINATE remediation as the other signing paths.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` as an edit target and specify that the `attest.refresh_attestation` call-site wraps `PlanRelationSnapshotError` into the standard unsigned INDETERMINATE remediation response.
evidence: orchestrator.py line 645: `sig = attest.refresh_attestation(ctx.ticket_id, cand['manifest'], probe='PASS', repo_root=repo_root)`
Plan: 'refresh_attestation gains no caller-supplied relation state' — it must call collect_plan_relation_snapshot itself
orchestrator.py is not named as an edit target in the plan

PlanRelationSnapshotError raised inside refresh_attestation must be caught somewhere — the plan says 'the review boundary catches it before any LLM call', implying the orchestrator call-site is that boundary
findings.finding: orchestrator.py is an unenumerated consumer of the refresh_attestation pin-wiring change: refresh_attestation is called at orchestrator.py line 645, and since refresh_attestation must now call collect_plan_relation_snapshot internally, orchestrator.py becomes an implicit integration point for snapshot-error propagation that the plan never names.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'refresh_attestation gains no caller-supplied relation state'
scenarios: If orchestrator.py does not catch PlanRelationSnapshotError from refresh_attestation, the error escapes to the caller as an unhandled exception instead of returning unsigned INDETERMINATE.
AC 11 ('Every unreadable or unresolvable target returns unsigned INDETERMINATE before any LLM call') would be violated on the drift-refresh path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale paragraph explaining why the O(store) full-preload approach was chosen: that it is the only single-pass mechanism for discovering inbound `blocks` edges from archived tickets, that it aligns with existing graph modules (_blockers.py, _unblock.py) using the same pattern, and that it eliminates N+1 reduce_ticket calls per target.
evidence: Plan states 'do not call find_inbound_relationships because its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets' — this is a rejection rationale, not a positive case for the chosen approach.
Plan states 'do not repeat store-wide reductions per target' — this is an efficiency constraint, not a positive rationale.

Existing codebase patterns in src/rebar/graph/_blockers.py, _graph.py, _unblock.py all perform the same O(store) reduce_all_tickets pattern, confirming alignment — but the plan never states this alignment as a rationale.
ABSENCE: no sentence in the plan begins with 'We choose the O(store) preload because…' or equivalent affirmative framing.
findings.finding: The plan lacks a stated positive rationale for WHY the O(store) full-preload approach (one store-wide ticket_states_by_id map built by reducing every ticket directory) was chosen as the collection mechanism. The plan explains what is excluded (find_inbound_relationships, repeated per-target reductions) but never affirmatively states the case for the full preload — e.g., that it is the only mechanism that discovers inbound `blocks` edges from archived sources in one pass, that it aligns with existing graph code (_blockers.py, _graph.py, _unblock.py) that already performs the same O(store) reduction, and that it avoids N+1 reduce_ticket calls per target.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 2: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states'
scenarios: An implementer reading this plan cannot distinguish a deliberate architectural choice from an accidental omission, potentially reopening the find_inbound_relationships debate during code review.
A future maintainer considering a lazy-resolution optimization (e.g., for stores with millions of tickets) has no documented rationale to consult before making the change.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation paragraph that introduces PlanRelationSnapshot, add: 'The O(store) preload is required because archived inbound-block sources cannot be discovered without scanning the full store (find_inbound_relationships filters them at line 103 of _inbound.py), and it avoids N+1 reduce_ticket calls per relation target. This matches the existing pattern in graph._blockers, graph._unblock, and graph._graph, which all perform one reduce_all_tickets pass for the same reason.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why tracker_head_sha() cannot use run_git() as its subprocess substrate (or refactor to delegate to it), to avoid reinventing the established git wrapper.
evidence: Plan: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) is new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u'
src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py:26 — run_git() is the single shared wrapper for exactly this shape
src/rebar/llm/fs_tools.py:104 — llm/ layer already invokes git subprocesses via the same pattern
Cache finding b6778c35 confirms: 'tracker_head_sha() in relation_snapshot.py rebuilds git subprocess invocation logic … that run_git() in rebar._store.gitutil already provides'
ABSENCE: no stated rationale for not using run_git() as the substrate
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() specifies new git subprocess invocation logic (status --porcelain, ls-files -u, rev-parse HEAD plus full error-handling) in a new module, reinventing run_git() which already exists in rebar._store.gitutil and is already used at the llm/ layer (rebar/llm/fs_tools.py line 104 calls git ls-files via subprocess; run_git is importable there). The plan acknowledges 'new strict logic rather than snapshot_head reuse' but does not justify why run_git() — the established single wrapper — cannot serve as the substrate, raising a repackaging concern.
findings.location: Implementation § tracker_head_sha (paragraph 5)
scenarios: Divergent error-handling contracts between tracker_head_sha() and run_git() callers could produce silent inconsistencies under future git-timeout or encoding changes.
Maintenance burden: two parallel git invocation paths for the same operations.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement tracker_head_sha() by calling run_git(tracker, 'status', '--porcelain', check=False), run_git(tracker, 'ls-files', '-u', check=False), and run_git(tracker, 'rev-parse', 'HEAD', check=False), catching OSError/CalledProcessError and re-raising as PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason='store-read-failure'). This keeps the single git wrapper contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why an O(store) full reduce is necessary rather than extending find_inbound_relationships (or a thin variant) to include archived sources via a parameter, given that the byte-scan already visits all directories.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py:103 — filter is `_INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES` (only 'deleted') OR `archived`; byte-scan in _mentioning_dirs visits all dirs
Plan: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states; this explicit O(store) map is required'
Cache finding 3afd5359: 'The plan mandates an O(store) full-preload … explicitly rejecting the established two-phase approach'
ABSENCE: no consideration of a parameterized/variant find_inbound_relationships that includes archived sources
findings.finding: The plan mandates an O(store) full preload of every ticket directory (including archived) to discover inbound block relationships, explicitly rejecting find_inbound_relationships. The sole stated justification is that find_inbound_relationships 'intentionally omits archived tickets'. However, _inbound.py line 103 shows the active-source filter is `state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived')` where _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES = {'deleted'} only. The byte-scan approach in _mentioning_dirs already visits ALL ticket directories including archived; only the reduce step skips archived. Removing or parameterizing the archived filter in find_inbound_relationships (or a thin variant) would achieve the same result at O(scan + reduce-archived-candidates) rather than O(store full reduce). The plan does not consider this established approach or justify why a full O(store) reduce is necessary over the cheaper alternative.
findings.location: Implementation § PlanRelationSnapshot (paragraph 2): 'do not call find_inbound_relationships because its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets'
scenarios: Large stores with many archived tickets will incur a full O(store) reduce cost on every plan-review signing, including the common case with zero archived prerequisite sources.
Future store growth makes this an unbounded per-sign latency regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `git ls-files -u` invocation and its stdout format against `git ls-files --help` (or an actual tracker run), and confirm the 'any output = store-read-failure' rule behaves correctly for the multi-column merge-conflict output format.
evidence: Search for 'ls-files -u' returned zero matches across the entire codebase (production and tests)
Partially-verified: git ls-files -u is a standard git plumbing command but has not been probed via --help in this environment
The plan asserts 'any output raises store-read-failure' — the precise stdout format (file paths, one per line) is unverified against actual tracker state
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'git -C <tracker> ls-files -u' as a new git subcommand invocation in tracker_head_sha() to detect unmerged files, but git ls-files -u is not used anywhere in the codebase. While -u is a standard git flag for showing unmerged index entries (Partially-verified from documentation knowledge, not observed in this repo), the plan does not verify this surface against --help output or confirm the exact stdout format the 'any output raises store-read-failure' rule interprets.
findings.location: Implementation section — tracker_head_sha() spec: 'run git -C <tracker> ls-files -u; any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError'
scenarios: If the tracker ever has an in-progress merge state (e.g. after a failed auto-merge in a CI environment), ls-files -u produces multi-column output (mode, sha, stage, path) rather than bare paths — the 'any output' rule fires correctly regardless of format, so the semantic behavior is sound, but the invocation itself has never been exercised in this codebase

If git ls-files -u exits non-zero in an edge case (e.g. outside a git repo), the plan's error mapping must catch it; this is covered by 'nonzero subprocess exits ... are all caught and re-raised as store-read-failure'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the normalization rule ('outgoing depends_on AND inbound blocks both → role=prerequisite') in the `relation_snapshot.py` Implementation section alongside the `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` definition, not only in AC 8.
evidence: Implementation bullet 2 says: 'Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries across that full map' — role assignment is never stated here.
AC 8 says: 'Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite' — the only place the mapping protocol is expressed.
The MANAGED_REF_KINDS in src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py line 50 confirms 'blocks' and 'depends_on' are separate, distinct relation kinds — making the ambiguity implementation-critical.
An executor implementing `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` from the Implementation section alone cannot determine that a `blocks` inbound edge maps to `role=prerequisite` without reading AC 8.
findings.finding: The normalization decision rule mapping both outgoing `depends_on` AND inbound `blocks` edges to role=`prerequisite` in `related_material` is stated only in AC 8, not co-located in the `relation_snapshot.py` Implementation section that defines `PlanRelationSnapshot` and its `related_material` field. An implementer building `relation_snapshot.py` from the Implementation bullet alone ('Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by scanning relation entries') learns what to collect but not the mapping rule for the `blocks` edge.
findings.location: Implementation bullet 2 (PlanRelationSnapshot definition) vs. AC 8 ('Both prerequisite relation directions normalize to role prerequisite')
scenarios: Implementer reads Implementation bullet 2 and the PlanMaterialPin definition ('Roles are exactly child and prerequisite'), writes `related_material` collection that maps depends_on → prerequisite but fails to map inbound blocks → prerequisite, instead treating blocks as a separate unknown role or skipping it.
Unit test for AC 8 fails because relation_snapshot.py was coded without the mapping rule.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation bullet describing `PlanRelationSnapshot`, add: 'Normalize edges: outgoing depends_on relations map to role=prerequisite; inbound blocks relations (from tickets whose deps contain the subject as a blocks target) also map to role=prerequisite. Both directions produce role=prerequisite entries in related_material.'

findings.finding: The plan assigns verify.enforce_plan_material_pins to child 75de, but also says 'tests toggle it through a temporary project config' in THIS story. If the config key does not exist in VerifyConfig (_config_schema.py) until child 75de lands, this story's tests cannot write it to rebar.toml and read it back. The plan never names _config_schema.py as an edit target for this story, yet the AC and test claims require the key to be readable.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph beginning 'Enforcement means the boolean config key verify.enforce_plan_material_pins'; Acceptance Criteria lines 17-18

findings.finding: The plan adds `plan-material-pin:` lines to the persisted manifest format and explicitly tests that a pre-feature VERIFIER can authenticate and ignore them, but it never asserts the post-rollback persisted format state for SIGNING operations. If old signing code (resign_plan_review, refresh_attestation) is re-deployed after new-format manifests exist, those operations will silently produce a manifest without pin lines, dropping pin data from the persisted artifact. The plan is silent on whether this constitutes acceptable data loss or what the canonical format state should be after such a rollback.
findings.location: Implementation §6 ('Extend build_manifest…Every plan-review signature-producing path…'); Verification §1 ('pre-feature reader authenticates…then ignores additive pin lines')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback section that states one of: (a) old signing code detects unknown manifest lines and refuses to re-sign (fast-fail, no data loss); (b) the feature branch is never partially deployed such that old code re-signs new manifests (deployment gate); or (c) pin-line loss on rollback re-signing is explicitly acknowledged as acceptable DATA LOSS RISK because pins are reconstructible from the graph on next forward-sign. Whichever path is chosen, add it to Acceptance Criteria with a matching test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Implementation section which field of the reduced ticket state constitutes the canonical ID for the mismatch check (e.g. the reducer-output `ticket_id` field), and what comparison is performed.
evidence: Plan says: 'A target is readable only when its reference resolves uniquely to an existing reducer-readable state with matching canonical ID'.
Plan says: 'canonical-id-mismatch' is one of the exact PlanRelationSnapshotError reason values.
The plan never specifies which field of the reduced state (e.g. 'ticket_id', 'id', 'canonical_id') holds the canonical ID to compare against the resolved reference.
Absence: no co-located schema or field reference that names the canonical ID field on a reduced ticket dict.
findings.finding: The `canonical-id-mismatch` error reason is referenced but the decision protocol — which field of a reduced ticket state constitutes the canonical ID, and how a 'mismatch' is detected — is never defined. The Implementation says targets are readable 'only when its reference resolves uniquely to an existing reducer-readable state with matching canonical ID' but does not specify which field of the reducer output carries the canonical ID to compare against.
findings.location: Implementation bullet 4 (target-readability protocol and PlanRelationSnapshotError reasons)
scenarios: Implementer of `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` cannot write the canonical-ID-mismatch check without guessing which reducer output field to compare — different implementers might use 'ticket_id', 'id', or another field, producing inconsistent behavior.

The canonical-id-mismatch AC ('Every unreadable or unresolvable target returns unsigned INDETERMINATE before any LLM call') cannot be unit-tested reliably without knowing what triggers the mismatch condition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md section or lightweight ADR capturing: (a) why pin lines are ordered after verified-at-sha and before dep lines, (b) why O(store) preload is required over incremental/active-source discovery, and (c) the pre-LLM error-boundary contract — so future stories (75de, 5967) can extend safely.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan

The plan introduces: (1) a new mandatory pre-sign collection phase that can abort signing, (2) a new manifest format extension with a defined sort order and collision rules, (3) a new O(store) preload pattern as the required discovery strategy — all of which are novel architectural decisions future maintainers will need to understand
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant novel architectural decision — a mandatory O(store) pre-sign phase, a new manifest format section (plan-material-pin lines), and a new structured error boundary before LLM evaluation — but does not specify any update to AGENTS.md, an ADR, or a design document capturing the decision rationale.
findings.location: Implementation section, overall plan; no documentation update mentioned anywhere
scenarios: A future maintainer extending the manifest format or adding a new relation role has no record of why pin lines must appear after verified-at-sha and before dep lines, or why O(store) preload was chosen over incremental discovery.
A future story (e.g., child 75de enforcement, child 5967 atomic signing) modifies the pre-sign boundary without understanding the design rationale, introducing subtle ordering bugs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Design Notes' or ADR entry covering: manifest format invariants (section ordering, role sort order, fingerprint source), the O(store) preload rationale (archived inbound blocker discovery), and the pre-LLM abort boundary contract. Reference it from AGENTS.md.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
